Maintenance & Reliability Engineering Report — Q1
2026
1. Maintenance Strategy Overview
SteelForge Manufacturing employs a three-tier maintenance strategy: Preventive Maintenance (PM),
Predictive Maintenance (PdM), and Reliability-Centered Maintenance (RCM). The Computerized
Maintenance Management System (CMMS) — SAP Plant Maintenance module — tracks all work
orders, PM schedules, spare parts inventory, and equipment history. Total maintenance spend for Q1
was $1.87M against a budget of $2.1M (89% budget utilization).
The ratio of planned to unplanned maintenance improved from 72:28 in Q4 2025 to 81:19 in Q1 2026,
exceeding the world-class benchmark of 80:20. Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) for critical
equipment increased 18% to 1,247 hours. Mean Time To Repair (MTTR) decreased 12% to 2.3 hours,
reflecting investments in technician training and spare parts availability improvements.
2. Predictive Maintenance Program

decreased 12% to 2.3 hours,
reflecting investments in technician training and spare parts availability improvements.
2. Predictive Maintenance Program
The PdM program covers vibration analysis, infrared thermography, oil analysis, and ultrasonic testing
across 156 critical assets. Key findings from Q1: Vibration analysis detected early-stage bearing
degradation on CNC Line 4 spindle assembly (Asset #CNC-A-004) in January, but the replacement
was deferred to the February maintenance window. The bearing failed catastrophically on Feb 8,
causing the 36-hour unplanned shutdown. This event prompted a revision of the PdM decision matrix to
lower the deferral threshold for spindle-class bearings from ISO 10816 Zone C to Zone B.

3. Spare Parts and Inventory Management
The spare parts storeroom holds $4.2M in inventory across 8,700 SKUs. Inventory turns improved from
1.8x to 2.1x through the implementation of min/max reorder points based on equipment criticality
ranking and historical consumption. Obsolete inventory write-off for Q1 was $127K, primarily related to
legacy Mazak control boards replaced during the CNC retrofit program. A consignment agreement was
established with SKF for bearing stock, reducing on-hand inventory by $310K while maintaining 98%
service level.

CONFIDENTIAL — M&A DUE DILIGENCE WORKING PAPER
Plant Manager + Procurement Manager only. Pre-decisional. Privileged.

ACQUISITION TARGET ASSESSMENT — PROJECT MERIDIAN
Target: PrecisionCore Bearings LLC (private, Ohio)
Prepared by: Corporate Development, in collaboration with Plant Manager
Date: April 2026

1. STRATEGIC RATIONALE
   PrecisionCore (PC) is a regional precision-bearing manufacturer with
   ~$28M revenue and specialty capabilities in P4-class spindle bearings
   that we currently source single-source from SKF. A successful close
   would:
     a) Eliminate the single-source exposure on CNC spindle parts that
        accounts for $1.1M of annual tariff risk.
     b) Bring spindle bearing manufacturing in-house, reducing landed
        cost by an estimated 22-28% on the affected SKUs.
     c) Add 18,000 sq ft of grinding and lapping capacity in Ohio that
        could absorb future precision-machining demand from our Line 5
        expansion (FY27).
2. FINANCIAL PROFILE OF TARGET
   FY25 Revenue ...................... $27.8M
   FY25 EBITDA ....................... $4.1M  (14.8% margin)
   FY25 Free Cash Flow ............... $2.6M
   Net Debt .......................... $0.4M
   Working capital % of revenue ...... 18%

M  (14.8% margin)
   FY25 Free Cash Flow ............... $2.6M
   Net Debt .......................... $0.4M
   Working capital % of revenue ...... 18%
   Pre-LOI valuation range:
     - 6.5x EBITDA (low / strategic floor) .......... $26.7M
     - 8.0x EBITDA (mid / negotiated base case) ..... $32.8M
     - 9.5x EBITDA (high / competing-bid scenario) .. $39.0M
   Recommended walk-away: $36M enterprise value (8.8x EBITDA).
3. SYNERGY MODEL
   Annual run-rate synergies, FY28 steady state:
     COGS reduction (in-house spindle bearings) ..... $1.6M
     Tariff elimination ............................. $0.8M
     SG&A consolidation ............................. $0.5M
     Capacity arbitrage (Line 5 ramp) ............... $0.7M
     ------------------------------------------------ ------
     Gross synergies ................................ $3.6M
     Dis-synergies (retention, integration costs) ... ($0.9M)
     Net steady-state synergies ..................... $2.7M

...................... $3.6M
     Dis-synergies (retention, integration costs) ... ($0.9M)
     Net steady-state synergies ..................... $2.7M
   Payback period at $32M EV: 4.1 years.
4. KEY DILIGENCE FINDINGS
   - Customer concentration is acceptable: top 5 customers are 41% of
     revenue, no single customer >12%.
   - Two key engineers (lead grinding metallurgist, P4-class process
     engineer) are critical-person risks; retention packages required
     pre-signing.
   - Environmental: one historical solvent release on the south pad
     (closed by EPA in 2018) — Phase II ESA recommends no further
     remediation. Reserve $0.2M for residual indemnity.
   - IT: legacy MES needs replatforming (~$0.6M, 6 months) post-close.
5. RECOMMENDED NEXT STEPS

remediation. Reserve $0.2M for residual indemnity.
   - IT: legacy MES needs replatforming (~$0.6M, 6 months) post-close.
5. RECOMMENDED NEXT STEPS
   a) Sign exclusivity LOI at $32M EV, 90-day diligence window.
   b) Engage outside counsel for SPA drafting (week of May 12).
   c) Pre-close briefing for Plant Manager + Procurement Manager only;
      operations team will be looped in after definitive agreement.
   d) Do NOT disclose target identity to shop-floor, planning, EHS, or
      buyer teams until public announcement. Standard M&A confidentiality
      protocol applies.

Plant Manager only. (Procurement Manager may view supply-chain section.)

LEADERSHIP SUCCESSION PLAN — 2026 REVIEW CYCLE
Houston Plant
Reviewed and approved: Plant Manager, March 2026

The succession plan is the most sensitive document the plant produces. It
identifies critical-role gaps, ranks internal candidates, and records the
compensation actions tied to retention. Disclosure outside the named
readership creates labour-relations risk, retention risk, and (in some
jurisdictions) compliance risk.

   Quality Director              Marcus L.          Ready-now  Low
   Procurement Manager           Open               n/a        HIGH
   EHS Director                  Sarah N.           Ready-now  Low

Ready-now  Low
   Procurement Manager           Open               n/a        HIGH

   Procurement Manager is the highest-risk open role; interim coverage is
   handled by the Sr Buyer with weekly check-ins from corporate. External
   search is in flight, target close: end of Q2.
2. HIGH-POTENTIAL EMPLOYEE WATCH-LIST
   The following individuals are tracked for accelerated development.
   Identification is strictly internal — public disclosure would harm
   peers' morale and create flight risk for the named employees.
   - Reliability Engineer (Lead)  → development path: Maint Rel Mgr in 18 mo
   - Sr Buyer                     → development path: Procurement Mgr in 12 mo
   - Quality Engineer (P&L lead)  → development path: Quality Director in 24 mo
   - Shift Supervisor (Line 2)    → development path: Production Mgr in 36 mo

     - Retention bonus pool ............... $480,000 (12 individuals)
     - Restricted-stock pool .............. 4,200 units (8 individuals)
     - Equity-cliff dates ................. staggered Mar / Sep 2027
   Detail by individual is intentionally NOT included in this file. The
   per-person amounts live in HRIS under role: hr_business_partner.
4. EXIT-RISK INDICATORS (Q1 2026)
   Three flight-risk signals tracked for the senior leadership team:
     - External recruiter contact frequency (HR pulse-check)
     - 360-feedback "engagement" trend (down >0.4 in any quarter = flag)
     - Sponsored-conference attendance (proxy for outside networking)

5. SUPPLY-CHAIN LEADERSHIP READINESS (for Procurement Manager)
   The Procurement Manager search prioritises:
     - 8+ years in industrial procurement, ideally bearing or precision
       metal-cutting categories.
     - Demonstrated experience reducing single-source exposure from
       30%+ down to under 25%.
     - Capability to inherit the SKF / Siemens / Sandvik master agreements
       (see "Strategic Supplier Pricing" working paper).
     - Willingness to relocate to Houston by Q3 close.

CONFIDENTIAL — STRATEGIC SUPPLIER PRICING & CONTRACT TERMS
Plant Manager + Procurement Manager only. Do not share with buyers below VP.

STRATEGIC SUPPLIER MASTER PRICING BOOK
Effective: Apr 1, 2026 – Mar 31, 2027
Owner: Procurement Manager, countersigned by Plant Manager

The pricing, rebate tiers, and contract terms below are the *negotiated*
positions with strategic suppliers. They underwrite the FY26 margin plan
and must not be disclosed to:
  - Operators or shift supervisors (who only need part numbers + lead times).
  - Buyers issuing routine POs (who see catalogue prices, not contract tiers).
  - External parties or auditors without legal review.
1. SKF NORTH AMERICA — BEARINGS & SPINDLE PARTS
   Master Service Agreement: signed Sep 2024, expires Aug 2027.
   Annual minimum commit ............... $3.0M
   Volume rebate tier structure:
     $3.0M – $4.0M  →  4.0% rebate, paid quarterly
     $4.0M – $5.0M  →  6.5% rebate, paid quarterly
     $5.0M+         →  9.0% rebate + 30-day extended payment terms
   Most-favoured-customer clause: yes (signed addendum 2024-11).
   Tariff pass-through: capped at 50% above 8% duty.
   Single-source SKUs: BRG-7203, BRG-7305-P4, SPN-CNC-12-SET.
2. SIEMENS INDUSTRY — SERVO DRIVES & PLCS

iff pass-through: capped at 50% above 8% duty.
   Single-source SKUs: BRG-7203, BRG-7305-P4, SPN-CNC-12-SET.
2. SIEMENS INDUSTRY — SERVO DRIVES & PLCS
   Frame Agreement: signed Jan 2025, expires Dec 2027.
   List price discount baseline ........ 22% off published list.
   Annual rebate (over $2.0M) .......... 3.5% calendar-year payout.
   Spare-part service level ............ 95% next-day for tier-A SKUs.
   Termination: 12 months written notice; liquidated damages cap $400k.
   Strategic note: qualification of Yaskawa as second-source is the only
   negotiating leverage we hold; do NOT signal weakness in renewal talks.
3. SANDVIK COROMANT — CUTTING TOOLS

3. SANDVIK COROMANT — CUTTING TOOLS
   Consigned-stock agreement: revenue-recognised on consumption.
   Discount off list .................. 28% baseline, 33% above $1.5M.
   Tool-life guarantee penalty ........ $0.05/insert below stated
                                         tool-life (rarely invoked).
   New-product trial window ........... 90 days, no obligation to buy.
   Confidentiality clause: pricing not to be shared with any party
   competing with Sandvik (i.e. Kennametal, Iscar reps).
4. PARKER HANNIFIN — HYDRAULICS & PNEUMATICS
   Renewal in negotiation — Procurement Manager to lead, target close
   June 2026. Internal walk-away: 25% off list at $1.2M+ commit.
   Backup: Bosch Rexroth (samples qualified, lead-time disadvantage
   of 3-4 weeks on tier-A SKUs — do NOT disclose this gap to Parker).

B combined, exceeding the 92% target. Total output was 1,847,500 units against a planned volume of

shutdown of CNC Line 4 in February caused by a spindle bearing failure. Preventive maintenance
schedules have been updated to include quarterly vibration analysis on all CNC spindle assemblies.

constraint. Plant B operated at 88.7% capacity, with the welding department showing the lowest

initiatives.

Quality Control Manual — SteelForge Manufacturing
1. Purpose and Scope
This Quality Control Manual establishes the procedures, standards, and responsibilities for ensuring
product quality across all SteelForge Manufacturing production lines. It covers raw material inspection,
in-process quality checks, final product testing, non-conformance reporting (NCR), corrective and
preventive actions (CAPA), and compliance with ISO 9001:2015 and IATF 16949 automotive quality
management standards.
All production personnel, quality inspectors, and line supervisors are required to follow the procedures
outlined in this manual. Deviations must be documented via the Non-Conformance Report
(NCR-Form-101) and escalated within 24 hours.
2. Incoming Material Inspection
All raw materials received at the loading dock must undergo inspection within 4 hours of delivery. The
Quality Lab team uses Acceptance Quality Level (AQL) sampling per ANSI/ASQ Z1.4 standard. Critical

undergo inspection within 4 hours of delivery. The
Quality Lab team uses Acceptance Quality Level (AQL) sampling per ANSI/ASQ Z1.4 standard. Critical
materials such as high-tensile steel coils, aluminum billets, and copper wire stock require 100%
dimensional verification using coordinate measuring machines (CMM). Chemical composition is verified
via optical emission spectroscopy (OES) against material test certificates (MTC).
Supplier performance is tracked through the Supplier Scorecard system. Suppliers with a quality rating
below 85% are placed on probation and require corrective action plans within 30 days. Repeat
offenders (three consecutive months below threshold) are escalated to the Approved Vendor List (AVL)
committee for potential removal.

3. In-Process Quality Control
Statistical Process Control (SPC) is mandatory on all CNC machining, stamping, and welding
operations. Control charts (X-bar and R charts) are maintained for critical dimensions. Process
capability indices (Cpk) must be ‡ 1.33 for standard features and ‡ 1.67 for safety-critical dimensions.
Real-time SPC data is collected via shop-floor terminals connected to the MES (Manufacturing
Execution System) and monitored by Quality Engineers on the SPC Dashboard.
First Article Inspection (FAI) is required for all new part numbers, engineering change orders (ECO),
and after any tooling change. FAI reports follow AS9102 format and must be approved by the Quality
Manager before production release. All measurement equipment is calibrated per ISO/IEC 17025 with
calibration intervals not exceeding 12 months.
4. Final Product Testing and Release
Finished goods undergo a battery of tests before shipment release. Mechanical testing includes tensile,

2 months.
4. Final Product Testing and Release
Finished goods undergo a battery of tests before shipment release. Mechanical testing includes tensile,
impact (Charpy V-notch), and fatigue testing per customer specifications. Dimensional inspection uses
CMM with GD&T; callouts per ASME Y14.5-2018. Surface finish is verified using profilometers (Ra
values). All test results are recorded in the Enterprise Quality Management System (EQMS) and linked
to the lot/batch traceability record.
Products failing final inspection are quarantined in the Material Review Board (MRB) area. The MRB
team (Quality Manager, Production Manager, Engineering Lead) reviews disposition options: rework,
use-as-is (with customer concession), or scrap. All MRB decisions are documented and require
customer approval for safety-critical components.
5. Non-Conformance and CAPA Process
Non-conformances are classified as Minor, Major, or Critical. Critical NCRs trigger an immediate

tical components.
5. Non-Conformance and CAPA Process
Non-conformances are classified as Minor, Major, or Critical. Critical NCRs trigger an immediate
production stop and 8D problem-solving process. Root Cause Analysis (RCA) uses 5-Why, Fishbone
(Ishikawa), and Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) methods. Corrective actions must be implemented within 15
business days for Critical NCRs, 30 days for Major, and 60 days for Minor. Effectiveness verification is
conducted 90 days after implementation.

INTERNAL RCA — BEARING FAILURE CASCADE ON LINE 3 (Oct–Dec 2025)
Owner: Maintenance Reliability Manager, reviewed by Quality + EHS
Status: CLOSED — corrective actions verified Feb 2026

1. INCIDENT SUMMARY
   Between Oct 14 and Dec 02, 2025, Line 3 experienced four unplanned
   spindle-bearing failures on CNC-09, CNC-12, and CNC-15. Cumulative
   downtime: 47 hours. Estimated revenue impact: $312k. No injuries.
   Two of the failures produced scrap that escaped to QA (subsequently
   contained by the daily SPC sampling).
2. TIMELINE
   Oct 14 — CNC-09 spindle seizure during finishing pass; teardown found
            scored race on the front P4 bearing (BRG-7305-P4 lot J49).
   Oct 28 — CNC-12 high-vibration alarm during qualification cut; same
            bearing SKU + lot, similar wear pattern.
   Nov 18 — CNC-15 vibration alert; preventive teardown confirmed
            initiating wear before catastrophic failure.
   Dec 02 — CNC-09 secondary failure 7 weeks after rebuild; root cause
            traced to a sub-optimal preload during the rebuild kit
            installation.
3. ROOT CAUSE (5-WHY)

e 7 weeks after rebuild; root cause
            traced to a sub-optimal preload during the rebuild kit
            installation.
3. ROOT CAUSE (5-WHY)
   Why-1: Spindle bearing race scoring under nominal load.
   Why-2: Loss of EHL film due to contaminated lubricant in the
          spindle reservoir.
   Why-3: Coolant ingress past the labyrinth seal.
   Why-4: Labyrinth seal o-ring set to obsolete spec (out-of-revision
          drawing dated 2018; current spec from SKF dated 2023).
   Why-5: Maintenance Engineering drawing pack not refreshed since the
          last SKF spec update; obsolete BOM line was not flagged
          during the spare-parts review.
   Contributing cause (Dec 02 recurrence): preload procedure on the
   rebuild kit was not updated for the 2024 SKF preload table; the
   installing technician used the original install-manual values.
4. CORRECTIVE ACTIONS (CAPA)

kit was not updated for the 2024 SKF preload table; the
   installing technician used the original install-manual values.
4. CORRECTIVE ACTIONS (CAPA)
   CAPA-2025-Q4-031
     - Replace o-ring spec across all CNC labyrinth seals (CNC-09, 12, 15).
     - Refresh maintenance drawing pack from current SKF release.
     - Verified: Feb 12, 2026.
   CAPA-2025-Q4-032
     - Update spindle-rebuild preload procedure to 2024 SKF table.
     - Re-train the four certified spindle rebuilders.
     - Add preload measurement to the rebuild sign-off checklist.
     - Verified: Feb 24, 2026.
   CAPA-2025-Q4-033
     - Introduce a monthly review of OEM technical bulletins to detect
       silent spec changes earlier.
     - First review: Jan 2026 (caught a Siemens drive-firmware note).
     - Verified: ongoing.
5. INTERNAL OBSERVATIONS (NOT FOR EXTERNAL DISCLOSURE)

- First review: Jan 2026 (caught a Siemens drive-firmware note).
     - Verified: ongoing.
5. INTERNAL OBSERVATIONS (NOT FOR EXTERNAL DISCLOSURE)
   - Lot J49 from SKF was within spec on incoming inspection; the
     failure mechanism was *secondary* (coolant ingress) not primary
     (defective bearing). Procurement has documented this for the
     next SKF QBR but the matter is closed; no warranty claim filed.
   - Two technicians completed the spindle rebuild course in 2022 and
     are *not* on the updated preload procedure. HR has been notified

     action externally — it is being handled inside the maintenance

   - Quality Engineering recommends a follow-up SPC review at the end

REGULATORY INCIDENT RESPONSE PLAYBOOK
Owner: EHS Director, reviewed by Plant Counsel
Revision: 2026-02

Purpose: govern the first 72 hours after a *reportable* regulatory event
(OSHA recordable injury, EPA-reportable release, hazardous-material
exposure above PEL, or any event triggering external notification). The
playbook codifies who decides what, how external communication is
controlled, and where the contemporaneous incident record is kept.
1. INCIDENT CLASSIFICATION (TRIGGERS THIS PLAYBOOK)
   Tier-1 (any of):
     - OSHA-recordable injury or fatality
     - Permit-required confined-space rescue
     - Hazardous chemical release above reportable quantity (RQ)
     - Process-safety incident with property damage > $25k
     - Any event reportable to EPA, OSHA, or state regulator within 24h
   Tier-2 (any of):
     - First-aid injury with restricted duty
     - Near-miss involving moving equipment + line-of-fire exposure
     - Permit-to-work non-compliance discovered during audit
2. FIRST 60 MINUTES — ON-SCENE PROTOCOL

nvolving moving equipment + line-of-fire exposure
     - Permit-to-work non-compliance discovered during audit
2. FIRST 60 MINUTES — ON-SCENE PROTOCOL
   1. Shift Supervisor declares the incident and isolates the area.
   2. EHS Officer is paged. If unreachable, on-call Maintenance Engineer
      assumes acting-EHS authority until relieved.
   3. Plant Manager is notified within 15 minutes (Tier-1) or end of
      shift (Tier-2).
   4. No statements to media, regulators, or families until Plant
      Counsel is briefed. Operators answering inbound calls follow the
      "no comment, the plant will respond shortly" script.
   5. Evidence preservation: do NOT clean the scene or move equipment
      until EHS clears photo, sketch, and witness-statement capture.

Workplace Safety & Environmental Compliance
Report
1. Safety Performance Overview
SteelForge Manufacturing maintained a Total Recordable Incident Rate (TRIR) of 1.8 for Q1 2026,
below the industry average of 3.2 for metal fabrication (NAICS 3323). There were zero lost-time injuries
(LTI) during the quarter, extending the LTI-free streak to 247 consecutive days. Near-miss reporting
increased 34% quarter-over-quarter following the launch of the anonymous digital reporting app,
indicating improved safety culture and hazard awareness among shop floor personnel.
The Days Away, Restricted, or Transferred (DART) rate was 0.9, well below the company target of 1.5.
All incidents were classified as first-aid only, primarily minor lacerations (3 events) and heat stress
incidents (2 events) in the heat treatment department during the February ambient temperature spike.

The annual OSHA compliance audit was completed in March 2026 with zero citations. Key compliance

ambient temperature spike.

STANDARD OPERATING PROCEDURE: CNC MACHINING CENTER OPERATION
Document ID: SOP-CNC-001 | Revision: 7 | Effective Date: 2026-01-15
Department: CNC Machining | Plant: Plant A
Prepared by: James Rodriguez, Manufacturing Engineer
Approved by: Sarah Chen, Quality Manager

This SOP defines the step-by-step procedure for operating CNC machining centers
(Mori Seiki NHX5000 and DMG Mori NLX2500) at SteelForge Manufacturing Plant A.
It ensures consistent part quality, operator safety, and equipment longevity.

Applies to all CNC operators (Grade C3 and above), setup technicians, and
production supervisors in the CNC Machining department. Covers operations for
aluminum, steel, stainless steel, and titanium workpieces.

- Mandatory PPE: Safety glasses (ANSI Z87.1), steel-toe boots, hearing
  protection (when noise > 85 dBA), cut-resistant gloves (ANSI A4 rating)
- Verify machine guarding is intact and interlocks functional before startup
- Confirm emergency stop (E-stop) buttons are accessible and operational
- Never reach into the work envelope while spindle is rotating
- Ensure coolant concentration is between 6-8% (check with refractometer)
- Lock out/Tag out per SOP-LOTO-001 before any tool change or adjustment

4.1 Machine Warm-Up
    - Power on machine and allow 15-minute warm-up cycle
    - Run spindle warm-up program (O9001) at 500, 2000, 5000, 8000 RPM
    - Verify axis home positions using reference marks
    - Check hydraulic pressure: 70-80 bar nominal
    - Verify coolant level in sump (minimum 75% full)
    - Check chip conveyor operation

4.2 Tooling Setup
    - Load tool list from job sheet (MES terminal)
    - Verify tool offsets using Renishaw OMP60 probe
    - Confirm tool life remaining in Tool Management System (TMS)
    - Replace any tools below 20% remaining life
    - Verify collet/chuck runout < 0.005mm using dial indicator

4.3 Work Holding
    - Select fixture per engineering drawing callout
    - Verify fixture locating surfaces are clean and free of chips
    - Torque fixture bolts per specification (typically 45 Nm for M12)
    - Verify part seating using air-sensing circuit (green light = seated)

5.1 First Article Run
    - Run first piece at 80% programmed feed rate
    - Perform 100% dimensional inspection per FAI checklist
    - Record measurements in MES quality module
    - Obtain Quality Inspector sign-off before production release
    - Retain first article as reference sample in labeled bag

Normal Production
    - Run program at programmed speeds and feeds
    - Monitor tool wear indicators on HMI display
    - Perform in-process gauging per control plan frequency:
      * Critical dimensions: every 5th part
      * Standard dimensions: every 25th part
      * Surface finish (Ra): every 50th part
    - Record SPC data points in real-time via shop floor terminal
    - Flag any Cpk < 1.33 to Quality Engineer immediately

ish (Ra): every 50th part
    - Record SPC data points in real-time via shop floor terminal
    - Flag any Cpk < 1.33 to Quality Engineer immediately

Tool Change Procedure
    - When TMS signals tool life expiry, pause program at safe point
    - Follow LOTO abbreviated procedure (SOP-LOTO-001 Section 4.2)
    - Replace tool and reset tool offset using probe cycle
    - Run single-part verification at reduced feed rate
    - Resume production after dimensional confirmation

- Run machine clean-up cycle (O9099)
    - Remove all workpieces and tooling from table
    - Clean chip pan and empty chip hopper
    - Top off coolant if below 80% level
    - Log production quantity and downtime in MES
    - Complete shift handover form (Form SH-201)
    - Report any machine anomalies on CMMS work request

| Symptom                    | Probable Cause            | Action                    |
|---------------------------|--------------------------|---------------------------|
| Chatter marks on surface  | Tool wear / wrong RPM    | Replace tool, verify RPM  |
| Dimension out of tolerance| Thermal drift / tool wear | Re-probe, check coolant   |
| Poor surface finish (Ra)  | Feed rate too high        | Reduce feed 10-15%        |
| Coolant foaming           | Concentration too high    | Dilute to 6-8% range      |
| Spindle alarm             | Overload / bearing issue  | Stop, call maintenance    |
| Chip evacuation problem   | Conveyor jam              | Clear jam, check auger    |

SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGEMENT PROCEDURES
Document ID: SCM-PROC-001 | Revision: 4 | Effective Date: 2026-02-01
Department: Procurement & Supply Chain | SteelForge Manufacturing
Prepared by: Maria Santos, Supply Chain Director

1.1 Purchase Requisition (PR)
    All material purchases must originate from an approved Purchase Requisition
    in the ERP system (SAP MM module). PRs are auto-generated by MRP for
    production materials based on BOM explosion and demand forecasting. Non-production
    purchases require department manager approval for amounts under $5,000 and
    VP-level approval for amounts exceeding $5,000.

Supplier Selection
    New suppliers must complete the Supplier Qualification Questionnaire (Form SQ-100)
    and pass an on-site audit scoring minimum 80/100. Evaluation criteria:
    - Quality Management System (ISO 9001 minimum, IATF 16949 preferred): 30 pts
    - Delivery Performance history: 20 pts
    - Financial Stability (D&B rating): 15 pts

Approved Vendor List (AVL)
    Only suppliers on the AVL may receive purchase orders. The AVL is reviewed
    quarterly by the Commodity Management team. Key suppliers by commodity:

    Steel (flat-rolled):
    - Primary: Nippon Steel Corp — Lead time 8-12 weeks, MOQ 20 tons
    - Secondary: ArcelorMittal — Lead time 6-10 weeks, MOQ 15 tons
    - Emergency: Steel Warehouse Inc — Lead time 1-2 weeks, premium +22%

    Aluminum:
    - Primary: Alcoa Corporation — Lead time 6-8 weeks
    - Secondary: Novelis Inc — Lead time 8-10 weeks

    Cutting Tools:
    - Sandvik Coromant — Consignment program, 48-hour replenishment
    - Kennametal — Standard PO, 2-week lead time
    - Iscar — Secondary source for specialty tooling

2.1 Classification
    Inventory is managed using ABC-XYZ classification:
    - A items (top 80% of annual spend): Weekly review, safety stock = 3 weeks
    - B items (next 15% of spend): Bi-weekly review, safety stock = 4 weeks
    - C items (bottom 5% of spend): Monthly review, safety stock = 6 weeks
    - X (stable demand): Standard reorder point
    - Y (variable demand): Dynamic safety stock with demand sensing
    - Z (sporadic demand): Order-to-demand only

Warehouse Operations
    Raw material warehouse operates 24/5 with 2 receiving docks and 3 shipping
    docks. Inbound materials are received through GR (Goods Receipt) process
    with barcode scanning for lot traceability. Storage follows FIFO (First In
    First Out) methodology enforced through WMS (Warehouse Management System)
    directed putaway and picking. Annual physical inventory count is conducted
    in December; cycle counts for A-items occur weekly.

gement System)
    directed putaway and picking. Annual physical inventory count is conducted
    in December; cycle counts for A-items occur weekly.

Kanban System
    Shop floor consumables and fasteners use a 2-bin kanban system. Each bin
    is sized for 1 week of consumption. When a bin empties, the kanban card
    triggers automatic replenishment via the WMS. Supplier-managed inventory
    (SMI) is used for high-volume fasteners with weekly vendor visits.

3.1 Inbound Logistics
    - Domestic steel shipments: Flatbed truck, 2-5 day transit
    - Import materials: Ocean freight via Port of Houston, 6-8 week transit
    - Expedited: Air freight authorized only by Supply Chain Director for
      critical production shortages (cost > 10x standard shipping)

3.2 Outbound Logistics
    - Standard: LTL or FTL via contract carriers (XPO, Old Dominion)
    - Customer-specific: Some OEM customers require dedicated carriers
    - International: Freight forwarder coordination for customs/duties
    - All shipments require ASN (Advance Shipping Notice) transmitted via EDI 856
    - Packaging per AIAG guidelines or customer-specific requirements

4.1 Risk Assessment
    Quarterly risk assessments are conducted for all Tier-1 suppliers using a
    Risk Priority Number (RPN) methodology. Factors include:
    - Geographic concentration risk (single-source, single-country)
    - Financial health monitoring via D&B alerts
    - Natural disaster exposure (earthquake, flood, hurricane zones)
    - Geopolitical risk for international suppliers
    - Cybersecurity posture assessment

4.2 Business Continuity
    Critical materials (steel, aluminum, titanium) must have minimum 2 qualified
    sources. Single-source items require 6-week safety stock buffer. The Business
    Continuity Plan (BCP) is tested annually through tabletop exercises simulating
    supplier disruption scenarios.

5. TROUBLESHOOTING PROCEDURES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

BELT TRACKING / MISALIGNMENT (ALM-C001, ALM-C002)

HOOTING PROCEDURES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

BELT TRACKING / MISALIGNMENT (ALM-C001, ALM-C002)
Probable Causes:
  a) Uneven loading on belt
  b) Material buildup on rollers or pulleys
  c) Belt splice not square
  d) Idler misalignment
  e) Take-up not centered

a) Uneven loading on belt
  b) Material buildup on rollers or pulleys
  c) Belt splice not square
  d) Idler misalignment
  e) Take-up not centered
Step-by-step procedure:
  1. Observe belt tracking while running at low speed (30 ft/min).
  2. Inspect for material buildup on head pulley, snub rollers, and return
     idlers. Clean with scraper if buildup found.
  3. Check that all carrying idlers are level and square to belt centerline.
     Use a string line along the conveyor frame as reference.
  4. Adjust snub rollers at head or tail:
     - Belt drifting right: move roller right end forward (in direction of travel).
     - Belt drifting left: move roller left end forward.
     - Adjust in 1/8" increments, wait 3 full belt revolutions between changes.
  5. Verify belt splice is square: measure diagonals across splice — should
     be equal within 1/4".
  6. Confirm take-up (gravity or screw) is centered and free to move.
  7. If belt continues to mistrack after all adjustments, belt may be
     damaged or stretched unevenly — schedule belt replacement.

d free to move.
  7. If belt continues to mistrack after all adjustments, belt may be
     damaged or stretched unevenly — schedule belt replacement.

MOTOR FAULTS (ALM-C003, VFD sub-faults)
Probable Causes:
  a) Motor overload due to belt jam or material buildup
  b) VFD parameter drift or corruption
  c) Motor winding degradation
  d) Insufficient cooling airflow
Step-by-step procedure:
  1. Check VFD display panel for specific fault code (F-codes listed above).
  2. For F07801 (overcurrent):
     - Inspect belt for jams or excessive material accumulation.
     - Check that discharge chute is not blocked.
     - Verify VFD current limit parameter (P0640) is set to 150% FLA.
  3. For F07900 (drive overtemperature):
     - Check VFD cooling fan operation.
     - Verify ambient temperature in drive cabinet < 40°C.
     - Clean VFD heatsink fins with compressed air.
  4. For F30001 (PROFINET loss):
     - Check PROFINET cable connections at VFD and PLC.

t < 40°C.
     - Clean VFD heatsink fins with compressed air.
  4. For F30001 (PROFINET loss):
     - Check PROFINET cable connections at VFD and PLC.
     - Verify network switch status (see PLC Reference, Section 6).
     - Ping VFD IP address from engineering workstation.
     - VFD IP addresses: CV-301: 192.168.1.31, CV-302: 192.168.1.32,
       CV-303: 192.168.1.33
  5. If motor winding fault suspected, perform insulation resistance test
     (megger) — minimum 50 Megohms at 500V DC.
  6. Reset VFD fault by cycling control power or pressing RESET on keypad.
     If fault recurs within 3 attempts, escalate to electrical maintenance.
SENSOR FAILURES

ault by cycling control power or pressing RESET on keypad.
     If fault recurs within 3 attempts, escalate to electrical maintenance.
SENSOR FAILURES
5.3.1 Belt Speed Sensor Failure
  - Symptom: Speed reading = 0 while belt is running, or erratic readings.
  - Check: Gap between sensor and target wheel (0.5-1.5mm).
  - Check: Sensor cable for damage or disconnection at terminal block TB-CV3xx.
  - Verify: 24VDC supply at sensor (measure at TB-CV3xx terminals 1 & 2).
  - Replace: Part HON-942-A4M, recalibrate PPR setting in PLC.
5.3.2 Belt Misalignment Switch
  - Symptom: False ALM-C001/C002 while belt is tracking correctly.
  - Check: Switch lever arm free movement, not stuck by debris.
  - Check: Switch contacts with multimeter (NC contact should read closed).
  - Adjust: Switch mounting position — lever should contact belt edge at
    1" deviation from centerline.
  - Replace: Part 4B-TS300 if contacts are worn.

djust: Switch mounting position — lever should contact belt edge at
    1" deviation from centerline.
  - Replace: Part 4B-TS300 if contacts are worn.
5.3.3 Photoelectric Sensor (Product Detect)
  - Symptom: Missed product detection or false triggers.
  - Check: Sensor lens for dust or debris — clean with soft cloth.
  - Check: Reflector alignment (retroreflective type).
  - Adjust: Sensitivity potentiometer on sensor body.
  - Verify: 24VDC supply and output signal at PLC input module.

SPEED DEVIATIONS (ALM-C004)
Probable Causes:
  a) Belt slippage on drive pulley
  b) VFD speed reference signal error
  c) Gearbox failure (internal gear wear)
  d) Motor slip exceeding nameplate

a) Belt slippage on drive pulley
  b) VFD speed reference signal error
  c) Gearbox failure (internal gear wear)
  d) Motor slip exceeding nameplate
Step-by-step procedure:
  1. Compare VFD output frequency to speed sensor feedback.
  2. If VFD output matches setpoint but belt speed is low:
     - Inspect drive pulley rubber lagging for wear (replace if <1/8" thick).
     - Check belt tension: gravity take-up should move freely; screw take-up
       should be adjusted to eliminate slip (belt sag between idlers: 2-3%).
  3. If VFD output does not match speed reference:
     - Check analog speed reference signal from PLC (4-20mA at AO-CV3xx-01).
     - Verify VFD analog input scaling (P2000-P2003).
  4. Check gearbox oil level and condition:
     - Oil should be clear amber; milky = water contamination.
     - Dark/burnt = overheating; metallic particles = gear wear.
     - If gear wear suspected, schedule gearbox replacement.

y = water contamination.
     - Dark/burnt = overheating; metallic particles = gear wear.
     - If gear wear suspected, schedule gearbox replacement.

8. SAFETY INFORMATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  - Never reach under a running conveyor belt. All work on belt, rollers,
    or drive requires LOTO per facility procedure LP-007.
  - Pull-cord E-stops are located every 50 ft. Know the nearest E-stop
    location before working near any conveyor.
  - Conveyor guards must be in place during operation. Do not bypass guard
    interlocks (Allen-Bradley 440G-MT Guard Locking Switch).
  - Pinch points exist at head pulley, tail pulley, snub rollers, and
    take-up. Maintain 24" clearance zone.
  - CV-301 is interlocked with hydraulic press HP-401. Verify both systems
    are in safe state before performing maintenance on either.
  - Maximum walking speed near conveyors: normal pace. No running.

  - Parallelism: 0.001" per 12" of bed width
  - Die height adjustment: Motorized, 0-6" range
  - Die clamp: Hydraulic die clamping system, 4 clamps per platen

5. TROUBLESHOOTING PROCEDURES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

PRESSURE LOSS (ALM-H001)
Probable Causes:
  a) Hydraulic filter clogged (pressure or return)
  b) Main pump internal wear
  c) Servo valve internal leakage
  d) Cylinder seal bypass
  e) Relief valve set too low or stuck open
Step-by-step procedure:
  1. Check pressure filter differential pressure gauge (mounted on filter
     housing). Replace filter if DP exceeds 35 PSI.
     - Pressure filter part: PKR-15CN-110
     - Return filter part: PKR-15CN-210
  2. Check system pressure at gauge PG-HP4xx-01 (main manifold).
     - Normal operating: 2200-2500 PSI
     - If below 1800 PSI, proceed to step 3.
  3. Check relief valve setting:
     - Isolate press (LOTO per LP-007).
     - Remove relief valve cap and adjust with Allen wrench.
     - Factory setting: 3000 PSI (do not exceed).
     - If relief valve is stuck, replace: Part PFM-RV-400.

f valve cap and adjust with Allen wrench.
     - Factory setting: 3000 PSI (do not exceed).
     - If relief valve is stuck, replace: Part PFM-RV-400.
  4. Check main pump case drain flow:
     - Install flow meter on case drain line.
     - Acceptable: < 2 GPM at 2500 PSI.
     - If case drain > 3 GPM, pump is worn — schedule replacement.
  5. Check servo valve leakage:
     - Command servo to null (0V signal from PLC).
     - Measure leakage at cylinder ports with valve in null.
     - Acceptable: < 0.5 GPM at 2500 PSI.
     - If excessive, replace servo valve: MOOG-D634-319C.
  6. Check cylinder seals:
     - Extend cylinder fully and hold pressure.
     - Monitor pressure drop: should hold within 50 PSI over 5 minutes.
     - If pressure drops rapidly, internal bypass — replace rod/piston seals.
SLOW CYCLE TIME (ALM-H005)

op: should hold within 50 PSI over 5 minutes.
     - If pressure drops rapidly, internal bypass — replace rod/piston seals.
SLOW CYCLE TIME (ALM-H005)
Probable Causes:
  a) Low oil viscosity (oil too hot)
  b) Pump flow reduced (wear or cavitation)
  c) Accumulator precharge low
  d) Tooling interference or misalignment
  e) VFD limiting motor speed

ot)
  b) Pump flow reduced (wear or cavitation)
  c) Accumulator precharge low
  d) Tooling interference or misalignment
  e) VFD limiting motor speed
Step-by-step procedure:
  1. Check oil temperature: should be 70-130°F.
     - If above 130°F, see Section 5.3 (oil overheating).
  2. Check accumulator precharge:
     - Relieve system pressure to zero.
     - Connect nitrogen gauge to accumulator gas valve.
     - Precharge should be 1500 PSI (+/- 50 PSI).
     - Recharge with dry nitrogen if low.
  3. Monitor pump flow at test port TP-HP4xx-01:
     - Expected flow at 1800 RPM: 20-23 GPM.
     - If below 18 GPM, pump volumetric efficiency is degraded.
  4. Check for tooling binding:
     - Remove workpiece and run press empty.
     - If cycle time normalizes, issue is with tooling/die alignment.
     - Check die guide pins and bushings for wear.
  5. Verify pump motor speed:
     - Across-the-line start: confirm 1770-1790 RPM with tachometer.
     - Check motor current: should be < nameplate FLA.

Verify pump motor speed:
     - Across-the-line start: confirm 1770-1790 RPM with tachometer.
     - Check motor current: should be < nameplate FLA.

OIL CONTAMINATION / OVERHEATING (ALM-H002)
Probable Causes:
  a) Oil cooler fouled or inadequate cooling water
  b) Oil degradation from moisture or particulate
  c) Internal leakage generating excess heat
  d) Ambient temperature too high

dequate cooling water
  b) Oil degradation from moisture or particulate
  c) Internal leakage generating excess heat
  d) Ambient temperature too high
Step-by-step procedure:
  1. Check oil temperature at reservoir thermometer and compare to PLC reading
     (AI-HP4xx-TMP, WIKA A-10 transmitter).
  2. Verify cooling water flow from pump P-203 circuit:
     - Check inlet temperature at oil cooler: should be < 85°F.
     - Check flow rate: minimum 10 GPM through oil cooler.
     - If P-203 is down, oil temperature will rise rapidly — reduce press
       speed or stop until cooling is restored.
  3. Take oil sample and send to lab for analysis:
     - Particle count: target ISO 18/16/13 (servo valve requirement).
     - Water content: < 500 ppm.
     - Viscosity: ISO VG 46 (+/- 10% of new oil value).
     - Acid number: < 2.0 mg KOH/g.
  4. If oil is contaminated:
     - Run portable kidney loop filter (3 micron) for 24 hours.
     - Re-sample and test before returning to production.
     - If water content > 1000 ppm, drain and replace oil completely.
  5. Check reservoir breather filter (PKR-BB2-06): replace if dirty.

roduction.
     - If water content > 1000 ppm, drain and replace oil completely.
  5. Check reservoir breather filter (PKR-BB2-06): replace if dirty.

POSITION ERRORS (ALM-H004)
Probable Causes:
  a) Temposonics transducer failure or drift
  b) Servo valve calibration drift
  c) PLC analog output error
  d) Mechanical interference preventing full stroke
  e) Air in hydraulic circuit
Step-by-step procedure:
  1. Check Temposonics transducer reading on HMI diagnostic screen.
     - Compare to manual measurement of ram position.
     - If readings disagree, recalibrate transducer:
       a. Move ram to known position (bottom of stroke = 0.000").
       b. Zero transducer output in PLC (parameter HP4xx_POS_ZERO).
       c. Move to full stroke and set span (HP4xx_POS_SPAN).
  2. Check servo valve command vs. actual position:
     - On HMI, view servo valve diagnostic page.
     - Command should track position within +/- 0.003".
     - If hunting or oscillating, adjust PID gains:

On HMI, view servo valve diagnostic page.
     - Command should track position within +/- 0.003".
     - If hunting or oscillating, adjust PID gains:
       HP4xx_PID_P (proportional, default 2.5)
       HP4xx_PID_I (integral, default 0.8)
       HP4xx_PID_D (derivative, default 0.1)
  3. Bleed air from hydraulic circuit:
     - Open bleed valves BV-HP4xx-01 (cylinder top) and BV-HP4xx-02 (bottom).
     - Cycle press slowly at low pressure (500 PSI) 10 times.
     - Close bleed valves when oil flows without bubbles.
  4. Check PLC analog output module:
     - Measure signal at AO-HP4xx-SV with multimeter.
     - At 0% command: should read 0.00V (+/- 0.05V).
     - At 100% command: should read +10.00V (+/- 0.05V).
     - If outside tolerance, replace analog module (1769-OF4).

LOCKOUT/TAGOUT (LOTO) PROCEDURE
Reference: Facility Procedure LP-007 — Energy Control Program
Energy Sources for HP-400 Series:
  Source                  | Location              | Isolation Device
  ------------------------|-----------------------|--------------------
  Electrical (460V 3ph)   | MCC Bucket HP-4xx     | Circuit breaker CB-HP4xx
  Hydraulic pressure      | Main manifold         | Manual dump valve DV-HP4xx
  Pneumatic (if equipped) | Air supply header     | Ball valve BV-AIR-HP4xx
  Gravity (ram)           | Ram/platen            | Mechanical block MB-HP4xx
  Stored energy (accum.)  | Accumulator           | Manual bleed valve ABV-HP4xx

y (ram)           | Ram/platen            | Mechanical block MB-HP4xx
  Stored energy (accum.)  | Accumulator           | Manual bleed valve ABV-HP4xx
LOTO Sequence:
  1. Notify all affected personnel of impending lockout.
  2. Place press in "Setup" mode on HMI and press E-stop.
  3. Open and lock circuit breaker CB-HP4xx at MCC. Apply personal lock and tag.
  4. Verify zero voltage at motor terminal box with voltmeter.
  5. Open manual dump valve DV-HP4xx to relieve hydraulic pressure.
  6. Verify pressure gauge PG-HP4xx-01 reads 0 PSI.
  7. Bleed accumulator: open manual bleed valve ABV-HP4xx. Verify gauge at
     accumulator reads 0 PSI on hydraulic side.
  8. Install mechanical ram block MB-HP4xx between upper and lower platens.
  9. If pneumatic diverters are present, close and lock BV-AIR-HP4xx.
  10. Attempt to start press from HMI — confirm no response (try-start).

latens.
  9. If pneumatic diverters are present, close and lock BV-AIR-HP4xx.
  10. Attempt to start press from HMI — confirm no response (try-start).
LOTO Removal Sequence:
  1. Inspect work area: all tools removed, guards in place.
  2. Verify all personnel are clear of the press.
  3. Remove mechanical ram block MB-HP4xx.
  4. Close manual dump valve DV-HP4xx.
  5. Close accumulator bleed valve ABV-HP4xx.
  6. Remove personal lock and tag from CB-HP4xx.
  7. Close circuit breaker CB-HP4xx.
  8. Reset E-stop on press.
  9. Perform test cycle in "Setup" mode (slow speed, reduced pressure).

SAFEGUARDING SYSTEMS
HP-401 (Forming Press):
  - Fixed perimeter guarding with interlocked access gate.
  - Gate interlock: Allen-Bradley 440G-MT Guard Locking Switch.
  - Gate must be closed and locked before auto cycle can start.
  - Gate lock release only when ram is at top-of-stroke and pressure is zero.

Switch.
  - Gate must be closed and locked before auto cycle can start.
  - Gate lock release only when ram is at top-of-stroke and pressure is zero.
HP-402 (Stamping Press):
  - Light curtain: Allen-Bradley GuardShield 450L, Type 4, Category 4.
  - Resolution: 14mm (finger detection).
  - Blanking: None configured (full beam field active).
  - Muting: Not applicable (no automated infeed).
  - Dual palm button: Both buttons pressed within 0.5 seconds to initiate cycle.
  - Anti-tie-down: Buttons must be released between cycles.

1. SCOPE AND PURPOSE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
This document serves as the central controls reference for all PLC-controlled
manufacturing equipment at the Greenfield Manufacturing Facility. It covers
controller hardware, communication networks, fault codes, HMI alarm management,
and network troubleshooting procedures.

Equipment covered:
  - Centrifugal Pumps P-200 Series (MAN-P200-REV04)
  - Belt Conveyors CV-300 Series (MAN-CV300-REV02)
  - Hydraulic Presses HP-400 Series (MAN-HP400-REV03)

Network architecture summary:
  - Primary PLC: Siemens S7-1500 (pumps, conveyors)
  - Secondary PLC: Allen-Bradley CompactLogix 5380 (hydraulic presses)
  - Gateway: Siemens-AB EtherNet/IP to PROFINET gateway
  - SCADA: Ignition by Inductive Automation, Version 8.1

Network Interconnection
The two networks (PROFINET 192.168.1.x and EtherNet/IP 192.168.2.x) are
physically separated. The only connection between them is the HMS Anybus
X-gateway, which translates between PROFINET IO and EtherNet/IP at the
application layer. There is no IP routing between the networks.
SCADA Connection:
  - Ignition SCADA server connects to both networks via dual NIC:
    NIC1: 192.168.1.100 (PROFINET network)
    NIC2: 192.168.2.100 (EtherNet/IP network)
  - Ignition uses OPC-UA for S7-1500 and EtherNet/IP driver for CompactLogix.
  - SCADA data historian: 90-day rolling storage, 1-second resolution.

Fault Code Details:
FC-001: CPU Watchdog Timeout
  - The S7-1500 CPU scan cycle exceeded the configured watchdog time (150 ms).
  - Cause: Excessive program complexity, recursive loops, or I/O delay.
  - Resolution:
    1. Check CPU diagnostic buffer via TIA Portal online:
       Online & Diagnostics > Diagnostic Buffer.
    2. Identify the OB (Organization Block) causing delay.
    3. If caused by communication delay, check PROFINET network (see Section 6).
    4. Restart CPU from TIA Portal or by cycling power.
    5. If recurrent, optimize program scan or increase watchdog time (max 500 ms).

Section 6).

    5. If recurrent, optimize program scan or increase watchdog time (max 500 ms).
FC-002: PROFINET Device Lost
  - A configured PROFINET IO device is not responding.
  - Cause: Cable fault, device power loss, device firmware crash, IP conflict.
  - Resolution:
    1. Identify which device is lost: TIA Portal > Online > Accessible Devices.
    2. Check physical cable connection at the device.
    3. Ping device IP from engineering workstation.
    4. If device is a VFD, check VFD power supply and control board.
    5. If device was replaced, assign PROFINET device name using PRONETA tool.
    6. See Section 6 for detailed network troubleshooting.

ntrol board.
    5. If device was replaced, assign PROFINET device name using PRONETA tool.
    6. See Section 6 for detailed network troubleshooting.
FC-003: Analog Input Out of Range
  - A 4-20mA input reads below 3.5mA (wire break detection) or above 21mA.
  - Cause: Transmitter failure, broken wire, loose terminal, incorrect scaling.
  - Resolution:
    1. Identify channel from PLC diagnostic (Module > Channel diagnostics).
    2. Measure current at terminal block with loop calibrator.
    3. If 0mA: wiring is broken — trace and repair.
    4. If signal is present but PLC reads error: check module configuration
       in TIA Portal (measurement range, diagnostics enabled).
    5. Replace transmitter if signal is erratic (see equipment manual for part).

FC-005: CompactLogix CPU Major Fault
  - Unrecoverable fault requiring program re-download.
  - Cause: Memory corruption, firmware bug, power glitch during write.
  - Resolution:
    1. Connect with Studio 5000 via USB or Ethernet.
    2. Go Online > Controller Properties > view fault details.
    3. Clear fault: Controller Properties > Major Faults > Clear.
    4. If fault persists, download project: Communications > Download.
    5. Reconfigure date/time and verify I/O mapping after download.
    6. Test press operation in Setup mode before returning to Auto.
FC-008: Gateway Communication Loss
  - The Anybus X-gateway is not passing data between PROFINET and EtherNet/IP.
  - Cause: Gateway power loss, firmware hang, configuration corruption.
  - Resolution:
    1. Check gateway status LEDs:

en PROFINET and EtherNet/IP.
  - Cause: Gateway power loss, firmware hang, configuration corruption.

    1. Check gateway status LEDs:
       - Module Status (MS): Green = OK, Red = Fault, Off = No power.
       - Network Status A (NS-A): Green = PROFINET connected.

    2. If MS is Red: cycle power on gateway (24VDC supply).
    3. If NS-A is off: check PROFINET cable to SCALANCE switch.

    5. If gateway was replaced, reconfigure using Anybus Configuration Manager:

       - Map data tags per Section 2.3 gateway data table.
    6. After gateway recovery, verify interlock signals:

6. NETWORK TROUBLESHOOTING PROCEDURES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

PROFINET NETWORK TROUBLESHOOTING

NETWORK TROUBLESHOOTING PROCEDURES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

PROFINET NETWORK TROUBLESHOOTING
Symptom: FC-002 (PROFINET device lost) or intermittent communication.
Step-by-step procedure:
  1. Identify the lost device from S7-1500 diagnostic buffer.
  2. Check SCALANCE X208 port status:
     - Access switch web interface: http://192.168.1.254
     - Navigate to: Information > Port Overview
     - Check link status, speed, and error counters for each port.
  3. Check cable continuity:
     - Use Fluke DTX-1800 cable tester.
     - Verify Cat6A performance (all pairs pass at 250 MHz).
     - Check connector pins for corrosion or damage.
  4. Verify device PROFINET name:
     - Use Siemens PRONETA tool (free download) to scan network.
     - Compare device name to TIA Portal project configuration.
     - If name is missing (factory reset occurred), reassign name using PRONETA.
  5. Check for IP address conflicts:

IA Portal project configuration.
     - If name is missing (factory reset occurred), reassign name using PRONETA.
  5. Check for IP address conflicts:
     - Ping each device IP from engineering workstation.
     - If two devices respond to same IP, resolve conflict in TIA Portal.
  6. Check PROFINET timing:
     - Send cycle: 4 ms (configured in TIA Portal).
     - Watchdog: 12 ms (3x send cycle).
     - If intermittent, increase watchdog to 20 ms as test.
  7. Review SCALANCE error counters:
     - CRC errors > 0: cable or connector problem.
     - Alignment errors: possible duplex mismatch.
     - Late collisions: network congestion or faulty device.
EtherNet/IP NETWORK TROUBLESHOOTING
Symptom: FC-006 (EtherNet/IP connection timeout) or intermittent communication.

twork congestion or faulty device.
EtherNet/IP NETWORK TROUBLESHOOTING
Symptom: FC-006 (EtherNet/IP connection timeout) or intermittent communication.
Step-by-step procedure:
  1. Check Stratix 5700 port LEDs:
     - Solid green: link up, no traffic errors.
     - Flashing green: link up, traffic active.
     - Amber: link up but port disabled or error.
     - Off: no link.
  2. Access Stratix 5700 web interface: http://192.168.2.254
     - Check port statistics for error counts.
  3. From CompactLogix, check connection status:
     - Studio 5000 > I/O Configuration > Ethernet module.
     - Connection status should show "Running."
     - If "Faulted" or "Inhibited," check RPI (Requested Packet Interval).
  4. Verify RPI settings:
     - HMI connections: 100 ms RPI (standard).
     - Gateway connection: 10 ms RPI (critical interlock data).
     - Servo valve: 5 ms RPI (motion control performance).
  5. Test connectivity:
     - Ping from engineering workstation to each device.
     - Expected latency: < 1 ms on local network.
     - If latency > 5 ms, check for network congestion or cable issues.

workstation to each device.
     - Expected latency: < 1 ms on local network.
     - If latency > 5 ms, check for network congestion or cable issues.

GATEWAY TROUBLESHOOTING (HMS Anybus X-gateway)

Symptom: FC-008 (gateway communication loss) or data mismatch between PLCs.

Step-by-step procedure:
  1. Check gateway power supply: 24VDC at terminals (+/- 10%).
  2. Check status LEDs (see FC-008 resolution in Section 4).
  3. Verify data mapping is correct:
     - Access Anybus Configuration Manager on engineering laptop.
     - Connect via USB to gateway.
     - Verify input/output byte mapping matches both PLC projects.
  4. Check data integrity:
     - From S7-1500 HMI: view GW_CV301_Running tag.
     - From CompactLogix HMI: view CV301_Status.Running tag.
     - Both should show same value. If mismatched, restart gateway.
  5. If gateway requires firmware update:
     - Current firmware: V2.15
     - Update via Anybus Configuration Manager (USB connection).
     - Backup configuration before updating.
     - Re-verify data mapping after update.

- Update via Anybus Configuration Manager (USB connection).
     - Backup configuration before updating.
     - Re-verify data mapping after update.

SCADA CONNECTION TROUBLESHOOTING
Symptom: FC-009 or FC-010 (SCADA connection lost to PLC).

fore updating.
     - Re-verify data mapping after update.

SCADA CONNECTION TROUBLESHOOTING
Symptom: FC-009 or FC-010 (SCADA connection lost to PLC).
Step-by-step procedure:
  1. Verify Ignition gateway is running:
     - Access: http://192.168.1.100:8088/web/status
     - Check system status page for active connections.
  2. For FC-009 (OPC-UA to S7-1500):
     - In Ignition Designer, check OPC-UA connection status.
     - Verify S7-1500 OPC-UA server is enabled (TIA Portal > CPU properties).
     - Check NIC1 (192.168.1.100) is connected to PROFINET switch.
     - Verify firewall rules allow TCP port 4840 (OPC-UA).
  3. For FC-010 (EtherNet/IP to CompactLogix):
     - In Ignition Designer, check Allen-Bradley driver status.
     - Verify NIC2 (192.168.2.100) is connected to EtherNet/IP switch.
     - Check CompactLogix connection limits (max 32 CIP connections).
     - Reduce polling rate if connection count is near limit.
  4. Restart Ignition OPC/driver module if connection does not recover:
     - Gateway webpage > Config > OPC-UA > Restart.
     - Or restart entire Ignition service (last resort).

5. TROUBLESHOOTING PROCEDURES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

HIGH VIBRATION (ALM-P001, ALM-P002)
Probable Causes:
  a) Misalignment between pump and motor
  b) Worn or damaged bearings
  c) Impeller imbalance (erosion, debris)
  d) Cavitation
  e) Loose foundation bolts

isalignment between pump and motor
  b) Worn or damaged bearings
  c) Impeller imbalance (erosion, debris)
  d) Cavitation
  e) Loose foundation bolts
Step-by-step procedure:
  1. Record vibration levels using portable analyzer (CSI 2140 or equivalent).
     - Acceptable: < 0.15 in/s velocity (overall)
     - Warning: 0.15 — 0.30 in/s
     - Critical: > 0.30 in/s
  2. Check foundation bolts for proper torque (3/4" bolts: 150 ft-lbs).
  3. Perform laser alignment check using Fixturlaser NXA Pro.
     - Parallel tolerance: 0.005"
     - Angular tolerance: 0.5°
  4. If alignment is within tolerance, check bearing condition:
     - Measure bearing temperature with IR thermometer.
     - Listen for bearing noise using stethoscope.
     - If defective, replace per Section 6.2.
  5. If bearings are acceptable, inspect impeller:
     - Remove casing cover per Section 6.1.
     - Check impeller for erosion, pitting, or debris.
     - Replace impeller if wear exceeds 1/16" on vane edges.

ove casing cover per Section 6.1.
     - Check impeller for erosion, pitting, or debris.
     - Replace impeller if wear exceeds 1/16" on vane edges.

SEAL LEAKAGE (ALM-P003)

Probable Causes:
  a) Seal face wear (normal end-of-life)
  b) Thermal shock from dry running
  c) Flush system malfunction
  d) Shaft runout exceeding 0.002" TIR

Step-by-step procedure:
  1. Isolate pump: close suction and discharge valves.
  2. Verify LOTO per facility procedure LP-007.
  3. Inspect seal flush system:
     - Verify flush flow: 1.5-2.0 GPM at gauge FG-P2xx-01.
     - Verify flush pressure: 15-25 psi above suction.
     - Clean strainer (Part: FT-STR-200) if clogged.
  4. If flush system is functional, replace seal cartridge:
     - Remove coupling guard and coupling half.
     - Slide back gland plate (4x M12 bolts).
     - Remove old cartridge and inspect shaft sleeve.
     - Measure shaft runout with dial indicator (max 0.002" TIR).
     - Install new JC-2100 cartridge per manufacturer instructions.
     - Set seal compression: 3/16" gap on setting clips.
     - Torque gland bolts to 25 ft-lbs in star pattern.

ridge per manufacturer instructions.
     - Set seal compression: 3/16" gap on setting clips.
     - Torque gland bolts to 25 ft-lbs in star pattern.

LOW FLOW / LOW DISCHARGE PRESSURE (ALM-P006)

Probable Causes:
  a) Suction strainer clogged
  b) Partially closed suction valve
  c) Air entrainment in suction line
  d) Worn impeller or wear rings
  e) Reverse rotation (after motor work)

Step-by-step procedure:
  1. Verify suction valve is fully open.
  2. Check suction strainer differential pressure (DP > 5 psi = clogged).
  3. Check for air leaks at suction piping flanges and gauge connections.
  4. Verify rotation direction: viewed from motor end, CCW for all P-200 pumps.
  5. Measure wear ring clearance:
     - Acceptable: 0.010 — 0.015" diametral
     - Replace at: > 0.025" diametral
  6. If impeller is worn beyond tolerance, replace per Section 6.1.

- Acceptable: 0.010 — 0.015" diametral
     - Replace at: > 0.025" diametral
  6. If impeller is worn beyond tolerance, replace per Section 6.1.

OVERHEATING (ALM-P004, ALM-P005)

Probable Causes:
  a) Insufficient lubrication
  b) Over-greasing (grease churning)
  c) Misalignment causing excessive bearing load
  d) Ambient temperature exceeding design
  e) Cooling water flow loss (P-203: check valve CV-P203-CW)

Step-by-step procedure:
  1. Immediately reduce pump load or stop pump if temperature exceeds 190°F.
  2. Check grease fill level: bearings should be 1/3 full.
  3. If over-greased, remove drain plug and run pump briefly to purge excess.
  4. Verify bearing housing is not insulated by debris or dirt buildup.
  5. Check alignment per Section 5.1 step 3.
  6. For P-203: verify cooling water supply from plant header is flowing.

ted by debris or dirt buildup.
  5. Check alignment per Section 5.1 step 3.
  6. For P-203: verify cooling water supply from plant header is flowing.

CAVITATION (ALM-P008)

Probable Causes:
  a) Insufficient NPSH available
  b) Suction lift too high
  c) Fluid temperature above design (reduced NPSH margin)
  d) Suction line restrictions

Step-by-step procedure:
  1. Measure suction pressure at gauge PG-P2xx-S1.
  2. Calculate NPSHa and compare to NPSHr:
     - P-201 NPSHr: 8 ft at BEP
     - P-202 NPSHr: 12 ft at BEP
     - P-203 NPSHr: 9 ft at BEP
  3. Ensure NPSHa exceeds NPSHr by minimum 3 ft margin.
  4. Check suction strainer for blockage.
  5. Verify fluid temperature is within design range.
  6. If cavitation persists, consider throttling discharge to reduce flow.

6. MAINTENANCE PROCEDURES AND INTERVALS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

PREVENTIVE MAINTENANCE SCHEDULE

Task                                  | Interval   | Procedure
--------------------------------------|------------|---------------------------
Visual inspection and leak check      | Daily      | Walk-down checklist WD-P200
Vibration reading (portable)          | Weekly     | Record in CMMS
Bearing temperature check             | Weekly     | IR thermometer
Seal flush flow verification          | Weekly     | Check gauge FG-P2xx-01
Coupling element inspection           | Monthly    | Visual, check for cracking
Suction strainer cleaning             | Monthly    | Isolate, remove, clean
Lubrication (grease bearings)         | Quarterly  | 2 pumps Mobil Polyrex EM
Alignment check                       | Semi-annual| Laser alignment
Seal replacement (planned)            | 18 months  | Full cartridge swap
Bearing replacement (planned)         | 24 months  | Both inboard & outboard
Full overhaul (casing, impeller)      | 48 months  | Complete disassembly

earing replacement (planned)         | 24 months  | Both inboard & outboard
Full overhaul (casing, impeller)      | 48 months  | Complete disassembly

BEARING REPLACEMENT PROCEDURE

  1. Perform LOTO per facility procedure LP-007.
  2. Disconnect motor leads and coupling.
  3. Remove bearing housing end caps (4x M10 bolts each).
  4. Extract bearings using SKF TMMA 100 puller kit.
  5. Inspect shaft for scoring or wear.
  6. Clean housing bore with lint-free cloth and solvent.
  7. Heat new bearings to 110°C using SKF TIH 030m induction heater.
  8. Install bearings with marked side facing outward.
  9. Pack with Mobil Polyrex EM grease (1/3 cavity fill).
  10. Reassemble and torque end cap bolts to 45 ft-lbs.
  11. Verify shaft rotates freely by hand.
  12. Reconnect coupling and perform laser alignment.

ALIGNMENT PROCEDURE

rque end cap bolts to 45 ft-lbs.
  11. Verify shaft rotates freely by hand.
  12. Reconnect coupling and perform laser alignment.

ALIGNMENT PROCEDURE
  1. Ensure pump and motor are at operating temperature (warm alignment).
  2. Mount Fixturlaser NXA Pro brackets on coupling hubs.
  3. Rotate shafts and record readings at 12, 3, 6, 9 o'clock.
  4. Calculate corrections for front and rear motor feet.
  5. Add or remove shims (stainless steel shim stock only).
  6. Re-measure and confirm within tolerance:
     - Parallel: 0.005" maximum
     - Angular: 0.5° maximum
  7. Torque motor hold-down bolts to 120 ft-lbs.
  8. Perform soft-foot check (max 0.002" lift per foot).

8. SAFETY INFORMATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  - Always perform Lockout/Tagout (LOTO) per procedure LP-007 before any
    maintenance activity.
  - P-202 handles chemical fluids — wear appropriate PPE including chemical-
    resistant gloves, face shield, and acid-resistant apron.
  - Verify zero energy state before opening any casing or piping.
  - Do not operate pumps with coupling guard removed.
  - Maximum casing temperature during operation: 120°C — use caution.
  - Report any seal leaks immediately to shift supervisor and environmental.
